• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Capstone Final Project, Summer 2026

Gender Differences or Schmifferences: Testing Replication with Open Source Data

Bradley Allen

Abstract

In 2011, a high rate of replication failure plunged the psychological sciences into a crisis of public trust. While methodological improvements over the last 15 years have begun to repair that trust, the emphasis on novel findings by publishers has placed constraints replication research. Because replication research is vital to progressing psychological science, the present study replicated past findings of gender differences in personality and experiencing stress, depression, and anxiety in a large sample (N = 38,885). Responses from the Answers to the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales data set were analyzed. Results replicated past findings that significant small to moderate personality differences existed between males and females, with females scoring higher on agreeableness and lower on emotional stability compared to males. Analyses of the relationship between personality and mental health indicators revealed emotional stability was the strongest predictor of mental health indicators, while personality better predicted stress, anxiety, and depression for males compared to females. On average, females reported significantly higher scores on stress and anxiety compared to males, with moderate effect sizes. Multi-group confirmatory factor analysis was conducted on the Depression Anxiety Stress Scale, which demonstrated full invariance across groups, thereby validating group mean comparisons. Finally, an ensemble model deferentially predicted physiological and behavioral symptoms of stress, anxiety, and depression [ADD ML FINDINGS HERE].

Intro

Insanity is doing the same thing over and over again, but expecting different results

– Rita Mae Brown (1983, p. 86)

To borrow from Rita Mae Brown, sanity is undercut when attempted replication yields different results over and over again. Indeed, failure to replicate psychological studies has led to a crisis of public trust in psychological science (Anvari & Lakens, 2018; Patil et al., 2016; Shrout & Rodgers, 2018). Some researchers have cited unpublished papers and publication bias as main contributors to the crisis, while others have cited poor experimental design, breadth of psychological subfields with varying research standards, and outsized media attention to replication failure (Bogdan, 2025; Johnson et al., 2016; Youyou et al., 2023). In recent years, methodological improvements have begun to repair the damage, yielding what has been deemed a “credibility revolution” (Korbmacher et al., 2023, p. 1). Despite the credibility revolution, there remains a strong need for replication targets (Pittelkow et al., 2023). Taken together, declaring a significant finding, then failing to replicate it reduces the credibility of scientific findings, and diminishes public trust, necessitating more studies of replication not only to repair the damage, but also to act as a sort of sanity check.

Replication

Researchers Nosek and Errington (2020) suggested that researchers are remiss to replicate because it is seen as “a boring, uncreative, housekeeping activity” despite replication being a “vital contributor to research progress” (p. 1). They argued such a mindset stemmed from the strict definition of replication as algorithmically following the steps of another researcher. Such a definition, they observed, may be impossible because observational research, for example, cannot be replicated exactly (e.g., an earthquake or election that has occurred implies the novelty of the experience for those who observed it is impossible to replicate). Rather than restricting researchers to an exact step-by-step model, Nosek and Errington offered a new definition of replication: “[A] study for which any outcome would be considered diagnostic evidence about a claim from prior researchers” (p. 2). They concluded that this definition reduced emphasis on study characteristics and increased emphasis on interpreting outcomes. Critically, it would grant researchers the methodological breathing room to reduce perceptions of replication as a boring and uncreative activity.

In addition to redefining the issue, researchers have called for methodological change when conducting novel research. Shrout and Rodgers (2018) recommended following open science conventions such a pre-registration to prevent post-hoc revisions to research questions or p-hacking. They further recommended improvements to power analyses, deeper consideration of effect sizes (e.g., heterogeneity of results due to different sample sizes in the replication study), and increased disclosure of nonsignificant findings. Koole and Lakens (2012) suggested that publications link replication studies to the original papers, effectively creating co-citations as incentives. Broad consensus among research psychologists is that replication is necessary to validate existing findings, legitimize the field of study, and bolster public trust (Shrout & Rodgers, 2018; Youyou et al., 2023). Given the stakes, as Amir and Sharon (1990) put it, replication research is a must.

To assist research psychologists in replicating results, the Open-Source Psychometrics Project (2019) makes available large, open source data sets, often personality-centric, for use in publications. The present study will analyze the Answers to the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales data set available from the Open Psychometrics (2019) webpage. This data set contains more than 39 thousand responses to the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS) 42-item measure (P. F. Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995), Ten Item Personality Inventory (Gosling et al., 2003), and multiple demographic variables, such as race, gender, and orientation. Given the breadth of available variables, the use of validated psychological scales, the large sample size, and the duration of data collection (i.e., 3 years from 2017 to 2019), this data set is ideal for exploring the relationship between personality, depression, anxiety, stress, and demographic variables (e.g., gender).

Gender Differences

When it comes to personality, stress, depression, anxiety, there are small to moderate differences between males and females. For example, females compared to males tend to report higher scores on personality traits agreeableness and lower scores in trait emotional stability (Lippa, 2010; Weisberg et al., 2011), where agreeableness is defined as a person’s tendency to seek social harmony and cooperation over competition or self-interest, while emotional stability is characterized by the ability to remain calm and resilient under pressure (Goldberg, 1990; McCrae & John, 1992). Emotional stability is often considered the strongest predictor of mental health indicators such as stress and anxiety for both males and females, though personality tends to better predict mental health indicators for males compared to females (Alizadeh et al., 2018; Uliaszek et al., 2010). Given these differences, the Answers to the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales data set is ideal for exploring gender differences in personality and mental health indicators (e.g., stress, depression, anxiety).

Present Study

The goal of this project is to replicate findings of gender differences in personality, depression, anxiety, and stress. With respect to depression, anxiety, and stress, this study will include multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) of the DASS 42- and 21-item scales to replicate findings of measurement invariance across gender (i.e., females and males). Target CFA studies include Gomez et al. (2014) and Lu et al. (2018), both of which found the DASS 42- and 21-item measures performed equally across males and females. Target personality studies include Lippa (2010) and Weisberg et al. (2011), both of which found females reported higher scores on trait agreeableness and lower scores on trait emotional stability than males and that these differences tended to be small to moderate. Finally, this project will attempt to replicate findings by Alizadeh et al. (2018) and Uliaszek et al. (2010) who found emotional stability was the best predictor for depression, anxiety, and stress for both males and females, and that personality better predicted mental health for males compared to females. Given methodological variations (e.g., large sample, open source analytic software and packages), this project aims to answer how well open source data replicates findings of gender differences in personality and experiencing stress, anxiety, and depression.

This project will further contribute novel findings by applying machine learning models to differentially predict moderate to severe depression, anxiety, and stress in males and females using the behavioral and physiological subscales of the respective to the DASS-21 scale. This will be accomplished using clinical cut-off scores established by S.H. Lovibond and Lovibond (1995) to form a binary of normal (0) and moderate to severe (1) symptomatic expression.

Method

Participants

The raw data contained 39,775 observations. For participant responses to be considered, they were required to pass an attention check. The attention check consisted of a 16 word list (e.g., boat, paucity, lucid), 3 of which were fake (e.g., cuivocal, florted, verdid). Participants were instructed to check a box next to words whose definitions they knew for certain. Participants who checked boxes for all 3 words were excluded from analysis, resulting in a reduction of 326 participants. Additionally, the replication aspect of the study required that participants identified as male or female. As a result, 540 participants who selected “other” and 67 participants who did not provide a response were excluded. Finally, a total of 7 participants who listed their age as greater than 100 were removed. The remaining sample size was 38,835. Demographic results are reported in Table 1. Additional demographic analyses can be found in the Exploratory Data Analysis section.

Table 1

Demographic Characteristics by Gender
# ====================================
# DEMOGRAPHICS TABLE
# ====================================

# 1. Generate counts, age statistics, and labels
summary_stats <- dass21 %>%
  group_by(gender) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    min_age  = min(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_age  = max(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_age = sprintf("%.2f", round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)),
    sd_age   = sprintf("%.2f", round(sd(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)),
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

f_n <- summary_stats %>% filter(gender == "Female") %>% pull(n)
m_n <- summary_stats %>% filter(gender == "Male") %>% pull(n)

age_rows <- data.frame(
  Variable = c("Min", "Max", "M", "SD"),
  Female   = summary_stats %>% 
    filter(gender == "Female") %>% 
    select(min_age, max_age, mean_age, sd_age) %>% 
    unlist() %>% 
    as.character(),
  Male     = summary_stats %>% 
    filter(gender == "Male") %>% 
    select(min_age, max_age, mean_age, sd_age) %>% 
    unlist() %>% 
    as.character()
)

# 2. Process Categorical Profile Blocks with clean string mapping
demo_clean <- dass21 %>% 
  select(gender, race, education, country) %>% 
  mutate(
    country = fct_other(country, keep = c("US", "AU", "CA", "GB", "ID", "MY", "PH"), other_level = "Other"),
    country = case_match(
      country, 
      "US" ~ "United States", 
      "AU" ~ "Australia",
      "CA" ~ "Canada", 
      "GB" ~ "Great Britain", 
      "ID" ~ "Indonesia", 
      "MY" ~ "Malaysia",
      "PH" ~ "Philippines",
      "Other" ~ "Other"),
    education = if_else(education == "University degree", "Undergraduate degree", education),
    across(everything(), as.character)
  ) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = c(race, education, country), names_to = "group", values_to = "Variable") %>% 
  filter(!is.na(Variable), Variable != "NA", Variable != "") %>%
  count(group, Variable, gender) %>% 
  group_by(group, gender) %>% 
  mutate(pct = round((n / sum(n)) * 100, 2)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(col_val = paste0(n, " (", sprintf("%.2f", pct), "%)")) %>%
  select(group, Variable, gender, col_val) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = col_val, values_fill = "0 (0.00%)")

# 3. Setup explicit template order sequences
country_order   <- c("United States", 
                     "Australia", 
                     "Canada", 
                     "Great Britain", 
                     "Indonesia", 
                     "Malaysia", 
                     "Philippines", 
                     "Other")
education_order <- c("Less than high school", "High school", "Undergraduate degree", "Graduate degree")
race_order      <- c("Arab", "Asian", "Black", "Indigenous Australian", "Native American", "White", "Other")

c_final   <- demo_clean %>% filter(group == "country") %>% slice(match(country_order, Variable))
e_block   <- demo_clean %>% filter(group == "education")
e_ordered <- e_block %>% filter(Variable %in% education_order) %>% slice(match(education_order, Variable))
e_other   <- e_block %>% filter(!Variable %in% education_order)
e_final   <- bind_rows(e_ordered, e_other)
r_final   <- demo_clean %>% filter(group == "race") %>% slice(match(race_order, Variable))

# 4. Programmatically compute row indices dynamically based on actual lengths
c_start <- 1
c_end   <- nrow(c_final)

e_start <- c_end + 1
e_end   <- c_end + nrow(e_final)

r_start <- e_end + 1
r_end   <- e_end + nrow(r_final)

a_start <- r_end + 1
a_end   <- r_end + nrow(age_rows)

final_table_data <- bind_rows(c_final, e_final, r_final) %>%
  select(Variable, Female, Male) %>%
  bind_rows(age_rows)

# 5. Set up dynamic top headers containing your centered group metrics
header_labels <- setNames(
  c(1, 1, 1), 
  c("Variable and Group", paste0("Female (n = ", f_n, ")"), paste0("Male (n = ", m_n, ")"))
)

# 6. Render table with headers
final_table_data %>%
  kbl(
    col.names = c("", "n (%)", "n (%)"), 
    # Align rows right for numbers, left for variable descriptions
    align = c("l", "r", "r"), 
    booktabs = TRUE #,
    # caption = "Demographic Profile and Age Characteristics Split by Gender"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16, 
    html_font = "Times New Roman", 
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
    latex_options = "striped", 
    full_width = TRUE
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "50%") %>% 
  column_spec(2:3, width = "25%") %>%
  add_header_above(header_labels) %>% 
  # Overrides the second level subheaders to center perfectly under the top labels
  row_spec(0, align = "c") %>% 
  pack_rows("Country", c_start, c_end, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Education", e_start, e_end, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Race", r_start, r_end, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Age (years)", a_start, a_end, bold = TRUE)

Male (n = 8679)

Great Britain 749 (2.48%) 378 (4.36%)
Indonesia 745 (2.47%) 132 (1.52%)
Malaysia 18085 (59.97%) 3401 (39.19%)
Philippines 590 (1.96%) 142 (1.64%)
Other 3582 (11.88%) 1802 (20.77%)

Less than high school 2876 (9.66%) 915 (10.68%)
High school 11111 (37.33%) 3576 (41.72%)
Undergraduate degree 11942 (40.12%) 2981 (34.78%)
Graduate degree 3835 (12.88%) 1099 (12.82%)
Race
Arab 235 (0.78%) 85 (0.98%)
Asian 19032 (63.11%) 3898 (44.91%)
Black 406 (1.35%) 168 (1.94%)
Indigenous Australian 13 (0.04%) 6 (0.07%)
Native American 143 (0.47%) 58 (0.67%)
White 6688 (22.18%) 3409 (39.28%)

M 22.99 25.05

Note. M = Mean; SD = Standard Deviation.

Materials

The Open Psychometrics (2019) data set Answers to the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales was explored. Observations included in the data were gathered through an online survey from 2017 to 2019, and recorded 39,775 responses. The raw data frame contained 172 variables, including 42 items for the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS) (P. F. Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995), 10 items from the Ten Item Personality Inventory (TIPI) (Gosling et al., 2003), 16 items for the attention check (i.e., one variable for each word), and a series of time related questions (e.g., how much time spent on each question, total survey completion time) and demographic variables (e.g., education level, college major, gender, religion, orientation).

Procedure

Adjustments were made to the DASS-42 and TIPI scores. The DASS-42 responses were recorded on a scale from 1-4, but should have been 0-3. This was corrected by subtracting 1 from each participant’s response. The scores were then summed and stored as depression, anxiety, and stress scores, respectively (P. F. Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995). Additionally, scores for each DASS-42 subscale were calculated and stored (e.g., dysphoria, anhedonia, inertia). From the 42-item DASS, the same scores were similarly calculated and doubled for the DASS-21 subset (S. H. Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995). Responses to TIPI questions 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 were reverse scored by subtracting each response from 8 (e.g., 8 - 1, 8 - 2, etc.) before taking the mean of each set and assigning them to their respective trait (Gosling et al., 2003).

Finally, user string data was extensively cleaned before exporting the data for analysis. User input of college majors were cleaned using the stringdist package (Loo, 2014), with the Jaro-Winkler algorithm enabled (Jaro, 1989; Winkler, 1990) to find the closes approximation of a specified major (e.g., user input of social psychology becomes psychology). The resulting standardized majors were grouped into 10 common categories (e.g., STEM and Natural Sciences, Health & Medicine). This data was only used for exploratory analysis due to the high likelihood of misclassification. To increase interpretability of the data, numeric values for gender, race, education level, and other variables were replaced with their respective strings (e.g., for race, 10 = Asian, 20 = Arab). Both the raw and cleaned data frames were used in the analysis, and filtered accordingly.

Measures

Depression, Anxiety, and Stress

Depression, anxiety, and stress scores were calculated using the 21-item Depression Anxiety Stress Scale (DASS-21) (S. H. Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995). Depression was assessed using a 14-item scale that included items such as “I felt sad and depressed” and “I just couldn’t seem to get going.” Anxiety was assessed using a 14-item scale that included items such as “I had difficulty swallowing” and “I felt terrified.” Stress was assessed using a 14-item scale that included items such as “I found it hard to wind down” and “I tended to over-react to situations.” Items for all three scales were measured using a 4-point Likert scale (0 = Did not apply to me at all, 3 = Applied to me very much, or most of the time). Descriptive statistics for each scale and subscale by gender are given in Table 2.

Table 2

DASS-21 Descriptive Statistics by Gender
# ====================================
# DASS DESCRIPTIVES BY GENDER
# ====================================

# 1. Map your raw subscale names to clean text
subscale_labels <- c(
  "depres21" = "Scale Total", "dysphoria" = "Dysphoria", "hopelessness" = "Hopelessness",
  "devaluation_of_life" = "Devaluation of Life", "self_depcrecation" = "Self-Deprecation",
  "lack_interest" = "Lack of Interest", "anhedonia" = "Anhedonia", "inertia" = "Inertia",
  "anxiet21" = "Scale Total", "autonomic_arousal" = "Autonomic Arousal",
  "skeletal_musculature_effects" = "Skeletal Musculature Effects",
  "situational_anxiety" = "Situational Anxiety", "anxious_affect" = "Anxious Affect",
  "stress21" = "Scale Total", "difficulty_relaxing" = "Difficulty Relaxing",
  "nervous_arousal" = "Nervous Arousal", "easily_upset" = "Easily Upset",
  "irritable" = "Irritable", "impatient" = "Impatient"
)

# 2. Process your raw data
dass_tab <- dass21 %>% 
  select(gender, stress21, depres21, anxiet21, matches("^sub21")) %>% 
  rename_with(~ str_replace(.x, "sub21_(.*)", "\\1"), starts_with("sub21_")) %>% 
  rename(
    devaluation_of_life = deval_life, 
    self_depcrecation = self_deprec, 
    autonomic_arousal = auto_arousal, 
    skeletal_musculature_effects = skeletal_muscle, 
    situational_anxiety = sit_anxiety, 
    difficulty_relaxing = diff_relaxing
  ) %>% 
  group_by(gender) %>% 
  summarise(across(
    everything(), 
    list(
      mean = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 
      sd   = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 
      min  = ~ min(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 
      max  = ~ max(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 
      n    = ~ n()
    ), 
    .names = "{.col}___{.fn}"
  )) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -gender, 
    names_to = c("subscale", ".value"), 
    names_pattern = "(.*)___(.*)"
  )

# 3. Extract n values dynamically
n_female <- dass_tab %>% filter(gender == "Female") %>% pull(n) %>% unique()
n_male   <- dass_tab %>% filter(gender == "Male") %>% pull(n) %>% unique()

# 4. Create the header vector safely to avoid the syntax error
header_labels <- setNames(
  c(1, 2, 2), 
  c(" ", paste0("Female (n = ", n_female, ")"), paste0("Male (n = ", n_male, ")"))
)

# 5. Reshape side-by-side, drop n columns, and align rows
wide_table_data <- dass_tab %>%
  filter(subscale %in% names(subscale_labels)) %>%
  mutate(
    mean_fmt = sprintf("%.2f", round(mean, 2)),
    sd_fmt   = sprintf("%.2f", round(sd, 2))
  ) %>%
  select(subscale, gender, mean_fmt, sd_fmt) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = subscale,
    names_from = gender,
    values_from = c(mean_fmt, sd_fmt),
    names_glue = "{gender}_{.value}"
  ) %>%
  slice(match(names(subscale_labels), subscale)) %>%
  mutate(Variable = subscale_labels) %>%
  select(
    Variable, 
    Female_mean_fmt, Female_sd_fmt, 
    Male_mean_fmt, Male_sd_fmt
  )

# 6. Generate the wide table layout
wide_table_data %>%
  kbl(
    col.names = c("Variable", "M", "SD", "M", "SD"),
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
    booktabs = TRUE #,
    #caption = "Descriptive Statistics for DASS-21 Subscales Split by Gender"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16,
    html_font = "Times New Roman",
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    latex_options = "striped",
    full_width = TRUE
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "40%") %>%
  column_spec(2:5, width = "15%") %>%
  # Pass the safely constructed named vector here
  add_header_above(header_labels) %>% 
  pack_rows("Depression", 1, 8, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Anxiety", 9, 13, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("Stress", 14, 19, bold = TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(c(1, 9, 14), bold = TRUE)

Male (n = 8679)

Impatient 3.23 2.15 2.91 2.17
Note. M = Mean; SD = Standard Deviation.

Personality

Personality scores were calculated using the Ten Item Personality Inventory (TIPI) (Gosling et al., 2003). Participants responded to the prompt “I see myself as” and rated items such as “Extraverted, enthusiastic” and “Calm, emotionally stable.” Extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, emotional stability, and openness to experience were all assessed using a 2-item scale, on a 7-point Likert scale (1 = Disagree strongly, 7 = Agree strongly). Descriptive statistics for each scale by gender are given in Table 4.

Data Analysis

Data Frames

After importing the raw and DASS-21 data frames, both were filtered to include only participants who passed the attention check, identified as either male or female, and whose age was less than or equal to 100. A third data frame was constructed to conduct CFA with the same filters, and one additional filter to extract the DASS questions (e.g., variables with a ‘Q’ prefix and ‘A’ suffix such as Q2A).

Software Packages

This project was coded in R (R Core Team, 2026) using R Studio (Posit team, 2026) with the following packages: broom (Robinson et al., 2026), effectsize (Ben-Shachar et al., 2020), kableExtra (Zhu et al., 2024), knitr (Xie, 2025), lavaan (Rosseel, 2012), naniar (Tierney & Cook, 2023), patchwork (Pedersen, 2025), purrr (Wickham et al., 2026), scales (Wickham et al., 2025), semTools (Jorgensen et al., 2026), tibble (Müller et al., 2026), and tidyverse (Wickham et al., 2019).

Missing Data

No patterns of missing data were found for mental health indicators (i.e., DASS), personality, or gender. Major category contained the higher percentage of missing data at about 35%, likely due to individuals who reported educational attainment not exceeding the high school level. The next highest percentage of missing data was in sexual orientation, amount to approximately 8%. There was less than 1.5% missing data in all other categories Fig. 1

Fig. 1

Missing Data
# Missing demographic
missing_demo = gg_miss_var(dass21 %>%
           select(country, source, education, urban, gender, engnat, age, 
                  hand, religion, orientation, race, voted, married, 
                  familysize, major_cat),
           show_pct = TRUE) +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"), 
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9)
    )

missing_demo

# ====================================
# Missing DASS
# ====================================

missing_dass = gg_miss_var(dass21 %>%
           select(matches("sub42"), stress21, depres21, anxiet21),
           show_pct = TRUE) +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"), 
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9)
    )

# ====================================
# Missing Personality
# ====================================

missing_personality = gg_miss_var(dass21 %>%
           select(extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, "emotional stability" = stability, openness) %>%
             rename_with(~ str_to_title(.)),
           show_pct = TRUE) +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"), 
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9)
        )

missing_traits = missing_dass + missing_personality
missing_traits

Methodological Variations

There are several methodological variations between the present study and target studies. The primary difference is in the analytic tools. Researchers in the target studies used software such SPSS and Mplus to perform statistical analysis, while this study used R Studio and various software packages (e.g., lavaan). Secondly, given the breadth of this project, some analyses were partially replicated. For example, Weisberg et al. (2011) conducted independent samples t-tests on an expanded set of Big Five domains (e.g., enthusiasm, politeness), while this study only explored the core traits (i.e., openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and neuroticism or emotional stability). Finally, this study employed multiple regression to explore the relationship between personality as a predictor of mental health indicators while Alizadeh et al. (2018) conducted Chi-square and multivariate analysis of variance to compare groups based on clinical cut-off scores.

Results

Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)

Multi-group (CFA) was conducted on the 42- and 21-item Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS) following Brown’s (2015) framework up to equal indicator error variances (i.e., strict measurement invariance) to ensure comparisons of group means were appropriate (Furr, 2018; Liu et al., 2017; Wu & Estabrook, 2016). CFA analyses were conducted in R Studio using the lavaan package (Rosseel, 2012), in accordance with syntax described in Hirschfeld and Brachel (2014) and Svetina et al. (2020). Six models were created: One model each for males and females, an equal form (configural) model (group = gender), an equal factor loadings (weak) model (group.equal = loadings), an equal intercepts (strong) model (group.equal = loadings, thresholds), and an equal indicator error variances (strict) model (group.equal = loadings, thresholds, residuals). Because the items utilize a 4-point Likert response scale, the data were treated as ordinal categories.

CFA Models for DASS-42 and DASS-21 Item Scales

Models were estimated using the Weighted Least Squares Mean and Variance adjusted (WLSMV) estimator which provides robust standard errors and fit indices suited for non-continuous, non-normal data (Finney & DiStefano, 2006; Hirschfeld & Brachel, 2014; Rosseel, 2012). Model fit was evaluated using standard psychometric cutoffs: a Comparative Fit Index (CFI) and Tucker-Lewis Index (TLI) of \(\geq .90\) for acceptable fit and \(\geq .95\) for good fit, a Standardized Root Mean Square Residual (SRMR) \(\leq .08\), and a Root Mean Square Error of Approximation (RMSEA) of \(\leq .06\) for good fit and \(\leq .08\) for acceptable fit (Bentler & Bonett, 1980; Hu & Bentler, 1999; MacCallum et al., 1996; Marsh et al., 2004). Full model results are reported in Table 3.

# ====================================
# DASS-42 MODEL AND ORDERED ITEMS
# ====================================

dass42_model <- '
  Depression =~ Q3A + Q5A + Q10A + Q13A + Q16A + Q17A + Q21A + Q24A + Q26A + Q31A + Q34A + Q37A + Q38A + Q42A
  Anxiety =~ Q2A + Q4A + Q7A + Q9A + Q15A + Q19A + Q20A + Q23A + Q25A + Q28A + Q30A + Q36A + Q40A + Q41A
  Stress =~ Q1A + Q6A + Q8A + Q11A + Q12A + Q14A + Q18A + Q22A + Q27A + Q29A + Q32A + Q33A + Q35A + Q39A '

items_42 <- c("Q3A","Q5A","Q10A","Q13A","Q16A","Q17A","Q21A","Q24A","Q26A","Q31A","Q34A","Q37A","Q38A","Q42A",
              "Q2A","Q4A","Q7A","Q9A","Q15A","Q19A","Q20A","Q23A","Q25A","Q28A","Q30A","Q36A","Q40A","Q41A",
              "Q1A","Q6A","Q8A","Q11A","Q12A","Q14A","Q18A","Q22A","Q27A","Q29A","Q32A","Q33A","Q35A","Q39A")


# ====================================
# DASS-42 MULTI GROUP CFA
# ====================================

# Define the cache file path
cache_file <- "rds_files/dass42_models_cache.rds"

if (file.exists(cache_file)) {
  
  # 1. Load the cache file if it exists
  # message("Loading models from cache...")
  cached_models <- readRDS(cache_file)
  
  # 2. Extract the models back into separate variables
  fit42_male       <- cached_models$fit42_male
  fit42_female     <- cached_models$fit42_female
  fit42_equal      <- cached_models$fit42_equal
  
} else {

    # ====================================
    # STEP 1: CALC. CFA FOR MALES
    # ====================================
    
    # Fit the model separately per group using WLSMV and Theta parameterization
    fit42_male <- cfa(model = dass42_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Male"), 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta"
                          )
    
    # View global fit statistics and parameter estimates
    # summary(fit42_sep_male, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 2: CALC. CFA FOR FEMALES
    # ====================================
    
    fit42_female <- cfa(model = dass42_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Female"), 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit42_sep_female, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 3: CALC. CFA COMBINED (EQUAL FORM)
    # ====================================
    
    fit42_equal <- cfa(model = dass42_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,       
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit42_equal, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # Save all environment variables into a single named list
    models_to_cache <- list(
      fit42_male       = fit42_male,
      fit42_female     = fit42_female,
      fit42_equal      = fit42_equal
    )
    
    # Save RDS Object
    saveRDS(models_to_cache, file = cache_file)
    # message("Models successfully computed and cached!")
}

# ====================================
# DASS-42 RESULTS TABLE
# ====================================

# Define the cache file path
cache_file <- "rds_files/fit42_table.rds"

if (file.exists(cache_file)) {
  # 1. Load the cache list if it exists
  cached_data <- readRDS(cache_file)
  
  # 2. Extract everything back into the environment
  fit42_male   <- cached_data$fit42_male
  fit42_female <- cached_data$fit42_female
  fit42_equal  <- cached_data$fit42_equal
  fit42_table  <- cached_data$fit42_table
  
} else {
  # 1. Define the specific fit indices you need
  selected_indices = c(
    "chisq.scaled", "df.scaled", "rmsea.robust", 
    "rmsea.ci.lower.robust", "rmsea.ci.upper.robust", 
    "rmsea.pvalue.robust", "srmr", "cfi.robust", "tli.robust"
  )
  
  # 2. Create a named list of your fitted models
  model_list42 = list(
    "M1: Single-group (Males)"   = fit42_male,
    "M2: Single-group (Females)" = fit42_female,
    "M3: Equal form"             = fit42_equal
  )
  
  # 3. Extract, combine, and format the data
  fit42_table = model_list42 %>%
    lapply(function(m) fitmeasures(m, selected_indices)) %>%
    do.call(rbind, .) %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    tibble::rownames_to_column(var = "Model") %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 3))) %>% 
    rename(
      `Chi-Sq (Scaled)` = chisq.scaled,
      `df (Scaled)`     = df.scaled,
      `RMSEA (Robust)`  = rmsea.robust,
      `RMSEA CI LL`     = rmsea.ci.lower.robust,
      `RMSEA CI UL`     = rmsea.ci.upper.robust,
      `RMSEA p-value`   = rmsea.pvalue.robust,
      `SRMR`            = srmr,
      `CFI (Robust)`    = cfi.robust,
      `TLI (Robust)`    = tli.robust
    )
  
  # 4. Save models AND the table into a single named list
  data_to_cache <- list(
    fit42_male   = fit42_male,
    fit42_female = fit42_female,
    fit42_equal  = fit42_equal,
    fit42_table  = fit42_table
  )
  
  # Save the combined RDS Object
  saveRDS(data_to_cache, file = cache_file)
}

# 5. View your final table
# print(fit42_table)

# print(modificationIndices(fit42_equal, sort = TRUE, maximum.number = 10))

# ====================================
# DASS-42 MULTI GROUP CFA ADJUSTED
# ====================================

dass42_adjusted_model <- '
  Depression =~ Q3A + Q5A + Q10A + Q13A + Q16A + Q17A + Q21A + Q24A + Q26A + Q31A + Q34A + Q37A + Q38A + Q42A
  Anxiety =~ Q2A + Q4A + Q7A + Q9A + Q15A + Q19A + Q20A + Q23A + Q25A + Q28A + Q30A + Q36A + Q40A + Q41A
  Stress =~ Q1A + Q6A + Q8A + Q11A + Q12A + Q14A + Q18A + Q22A + Q27A + Q29A + Q32A + Q33A + Q35A + Q39A
  Q7A ~~ Q41A
  Q21A ~~ Q38A
  Q17A ~~ Q34A
  Q10A ~~ Q37A
  Q32A ~~ Q35A '

# Define the cache file path
cache_file <- "rds_files/dass42_adjusted_models_cache.rds"

if (file.exists(cache_file)) {
  
  # 1. Load the cache file if it exists
  # message("Loading models from cache...")
  cached_models <- readRDS(cache_file)
  
  # 2. Extract the models back into separate variables
  fit42adj_male       <- cached_models$fit42adj_male
  fit42adj_female     <- cached_models$fit42adj_female
  fit42adj_equal      <- cached_models$fit42adj_equal
  fit42adj_loadings   <- cached_models$fit42adj_loadings
  fit42adj_intercepts <- cached_models$fit42adj_intercepts
  fit42adj_residuals  <- cached_models$fit42adj_residuals
  
} else {

    # ====================================
    # STEP 1: CALC. CFA FOR MALES
    # ====================================
    
    # Fit the model separately per group using WLSMV and Theta parameterization
    fit42adj_male <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Male"), 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta"
                          )
    
    # View global fit statistics and parameter estimates
    # summary(fit42adj_sep_male, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 2: CALC. CFA FOR FEMALES
    # ====================================
    
    fit42adj_female <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Female"), 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit42adj_sep_female, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 3: CALC. CFA COMBINED (EQUAL FORM)
    # ====================================
    
    fit42adj_equal <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,       
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit42adj_equal, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 4: EQUAL FACTOR LOADINGS
    # ====================================
    
    fit42adj_loadings <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model,
                              data = cleaned_data,
                              ordered = items_42,
                              group = "gender",
                              group.equal = "loadings",
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # # summary(fit42adj_loadings, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 5: EQUAL INDICATOR INTERCEPTS
    # ====================================
    
    fit42adj_intercepts <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              group.equal = c("loadings", "thresholds"),
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta"
                          )
    # summary(fit42adj_intercepts, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 6: EQUAL INDICATOR ERROR VARIANCES
    # ====================================
    
    fit42adj_residuals <- cfa(model = dass42_adjusted_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_42,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              group.equal = c("loadings", "thresholds", "residuals"),
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # summary(fit42adj_residuals, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # Save all environment variables into a single named list
    models_to_cache <- list(
      fit42adj_male       = fit42adj_male,
      fit42adj_female     = fit42adj_female,
      fit42adj_equal      = fit42adj_equal,
      fit42adj_loadings   = fit42adj_loadings,
      fit42adj_intercepts = fit42adj_intercepts,
      fit42adj_residuals  = fit42adj_residuals
    )
    
    # Save RDS Object
    saveRDS(models_to_cache, file = cache_file)
    # message("Models successfully computed and cached!")
}

The initial baseline CFA for the DASS-42 revealed structural strain \((\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .881, \text{TLI}_{\text{robust}} = .875)\). Examination of the modification indices indicated substantial unmodeled residual covariance between similar items within the same subscales (e.g., trembling items Q7A and Q41A; meaningless/empty life items Q21A and Q38A). After specifying five local residual error covariances to absorb this method effects variance, the adjusted DASS-42 model achieved acceptable fit \((\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .910, \text{TLI}_{\text{robust}} = .905, \text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .063, \text{SRMR} = .039)\).

# ====================================
# DASS-21 MODEL AND ORDERED ITEMS
# ====================================

dass21_model <- '
  Depression =~ Q3A + Q5A + Q10A + Q13A + Q16A + Q17A + Q21A
  Anxiety =~ Q2A + Q4A + Q7A + Q9A + Q15A + Q19A + Q20A
  Stress =~ Q1A + Q6A + Q8A + Q11A + Q12A + Q14A + Q18A
'

items_21 <- c("Q3A","Q5A","Q10A","Q13A","Q16A","Q17A","Q21A",
              "Q2A","Q4A","Q7A","Q9A","Q15A","Q19A","Q20A",
              "Q1A","Q6A","Q8A","Q11A","Q12A","Q14A","Q18A")

# ====================================
# DASS-21 MULTI GROUP CFA
# ====================================

# Define the cache file path
cache_file <- "rds_files/dass21_models_cache.rds"

if (file.exists(cache_file)) {
  
  # 1. Load the cache file if it exists
  # message("Loading models from cache...")
  cached_models <- readRDS(cache_file)
  
  # 2. Extract the models back into separate variables
  fit21_male       <- cached_models$fit21_male
  fit21_female     <- cached_models$fit21_female
  fit21_equal      <- cached_models$fit21_equal
  fit21_loadings   <- cached_models$fit21_loadings
  fit21_intercepts <- cached_models$fit21_intercepts
  fit21_residuals  <- cached_models$fit21_residuals
  
} else {

    # ====================================
    # STEP 1: CALC. CFA FOR MALES
    # ====================================
    
    # Fit the model separately per group using WLSMV and Theta parameterization
    fit21_male <- cfa(model = dass21_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Male"), 
                              ordered = items_21,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta"
                          )
    
    # View global fit statistics and parameter estimates
    # summary(fit21_sep_male, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 2: CALC. CFA FOR FEMALES
    # ====================================
    
    fit21_female <- cfa(model = dass21_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data %>% filter(gender == "Female"), 
                              ordered = items_21,
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit21_sep_female, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 3: CALC. CFA COMBINED (EQUAL FORM)
    # ====================================
    
    fit21_equal <- cfa(model = dass21_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_21,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,       
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # Full model summary
    # summary(fit21_equal, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 4: EQUAL FACTOR LOADINGS
    # ====================================
    
    fit21_loadings <- cfa(model = dass21_model,
                              data = cleaned_data,
                              ordered = items_21,
                              group = "gender",
                              group.equal = "loadings",
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # # summary(fit21_loadings, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 5: EQUAL INDICATOR INTERCEPTS
    # ====================================
    
    fit21_intercepts <- cfa(model = dass21_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_21,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              group.equal = c("loadings", "thresholds"),
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta"
                          )
    # summary(fit21_intercepts, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # ====================================
    # STEP 6: EQUAL INDICATOR ERROR VARIANCES
    # ====================================
    
    fit21_residuals <- cfa(model = dass21_model, 
                              data = cleaned_data, 
                              ordered = items_21,
                              group = "gender",
                              meanstructure = TRUE,
                              group.equal = c("loadings", "thresholds", "residuals"),
                              estimator = "WLSMV",
                              parameterization = "theta")
    
    # summary(fit21_residuals, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE, modindices = TRUE)
    
    # Save all environment variables into a single named list
    models_to_cache <- list(
      fit21_male       = fit21_male,
      fit21_female     = fit21_female,
      fit21_equal      = fit21_equal,
      fit21_loadings   = fit21_loadings,
      fit21_intercepts = fit21_intercepts,
      fit21_residuals  = fit21_residuals
    )
    
    # Save RDS Object
    saveRDS(models_to_cache, file = cache_file)
    # message("Models successfully computed and cached!")
}

Conversely, the DASS-21 baseline model achieved similar results without any post-hoc modifications \((\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .932, \text{TLI}_{\text{robust}} = .923, \text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .073, \text{SRMR} = .038)\). Furthermore, based on cut-off scores established by Nunnally (1994), the subscales demonstrated good \((\alpha \geq .80)\) model-based internal consistency using McDonald’s omega (McDonald, 1999), yielding coefficients of \(\omega = .92\) for Depression, \(\omega = .84\) for Anxiety, and \(\omega = .87\) for Stress. This approach was preferred over Cronbach’s alpha because coefficient omega provides a more accurate estimate of internal consistency when handling categorical data and when the assumption of tau-equivalence is violated (Hayes & Coutts, 2020; McNeish, 2018).

Due to the large sample size (\(N = 38,835\)), the Satorra-Bentler scaled chi-square difference test (\(\Delta\chi^2\)) is highly oversensitive to trivial group variations and expectedly yielded statistically significant results. Therefore, invariance was determined by tracking changes in the comparative fit index (\(\Delta\text{CFI}\)), where a value \(\leq .010\) indicates that invariance holds (Chen, 2007; Cheung & Rensvold, 2002). For the DASS-21, configural invariance was established (\(\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .932\), \(\text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .073\)). The highly constrained equal indicator error variances (strict) model fit the data equally well (\(\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .932\), \(\text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .071\)). The resulting change in fit was negligible (\(\Delta\text{CFI} = 0\), \(\Delta\text{RMSEA} = -.002\)), confirming full scalar invariance across genders. For the adjusted DASS-42, the equal form (configural) model (\(\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .910\), \(\text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .063\)) was statistically compared against the constrained equal indicator error variances (strict) model (\(\text{CFI}_{\text{robust}} = .910\), \(\text{RMSEA}_{\text{robust}} = .062\)). Mirroring the short-form findings, the fit shift was effectively zero (\(\Delta\text{CFI} = 0\), \(\Delta\text{RMSEA} = -.001\)), establishing full scalar invariance for the 42-item scale. Collectively, these invariance findings verify that item mechanics, factor structures, and category thresholds mean the same thing to both men and women, validating subsequent comparisons of latent factor means or observed manifest sum scores.

Because the DASS-21 performed equally to the DASS-42, subsequent analyses will be conducted using the shorter DASS-21 scale to compare observed means on depression, anxiety, and stress.

Table 3

Comparison of Model Fit Indices for DASS-42 Adjusted and DASS-21 Models
# ====================================
# DASS-42 ADJUSTED TABLE DATA
# ====================================

# Define the RDS file path
rds_path <- "rds_files/fit42adj_table.rds"

# Check if the RDS file already exists
if (file.exists(rds_path)) {
  
  # Load the existing table from the file
  fit42adj_table <- readRDS(rds_path)
  # message("Loaded existing table from RDS file.")
  
} else {
  
  # 1. Define the specific fit indices you need
  selected_indices <- c(
    "chisq.scaled", "df.scaled", "rmsea.robust", 
    "rmsea.ci.lower.robust", "rmsea.ci.upper.robust", 
    "rmsea.pvalue.robust", "srmr", "cfi.robust", "tli.robust"
  )

  # 2. Create a named list of your fitted models
  model_list42adj <- list(
    "M1: Single-group (Males)"              = fit42adj_male,
    "M2: Single-group (Females)"            = fit42adj_female,
    "M3: Equal form"                        = fit42adj_equal,
    "M4: Equal factor loadings"             = fit42adj_loadings,
    "M5: Equal Intercepts"                  = fit42adj_intercepts,
    "M6: Equal indicator error variances"   = fit42adj_residuals
  )

  # 3. Extract, combine, and format the data
  fit42adj_table <- model_list42adj %>% 
    # Extract fit measures for each model in the list
    lapply(function(m) fitmeasures(m, selected_indices)) %>% 
    # Bind the vector results into rows of a data frame
    do.call(rbind, .) %>% 
    # Convert to a standard data frame
    as.data.frame() %>% 
    # Move the model names from row names to an actual column
    tibble::rownames_to_column(var = "Model") %>% 
    # Optional: Round all numeric metrics to 3 decimal places for readability
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 3))) %>%
    mutate(chisq.scaled = round(chisq.scaled, 1)) %>%
    rename(
      `Chi-Sq (Scaled)` = chisq.scaled,
      `df (Scaled)`     = df.scaled,
      `RMSEA (Robust)`  = rmsea.robust,
      `RMSEA CI LL`     = rmsea.ci.lower.robust,
      `RMSEA CI UL`     = rmsea.ci.upper.robust,
      `RMSEA p-value`   = rmsea.pvalue.robust,
      `SRMR`            = srmr,
      `CFI (Robust)`    = cfi.robust,
      `TLI (Robust)`    = tli.robust
    )
  
  # Save the newly created table to the RDS file
  saveRDS(fit42adj_table, file = rds_path)
  # message("Generated new table and saved to RDS file.")
}

# ====================================
# DASS-21 TABLE DATA
# ====================================

# Define the RDS directory path
rds_path_21 <- "rds_files/fit21_table.rds" 

# Check if the RDS file already exists
if (file.exists(rds_path_21)) { 
  
  # Load the existing table from the file
  fit21_table <- readRDS(rds_path_21)
  # message("Loaded existing fit21 table from RDS file.")
  
} else { 
  
  # 1. Define the specific fit indices you need
  selected_indices <- c(
    "chisq.scaled", "df.scaled", "rmsea.robust", 
    "rmsea.ci.lower.robust", "rmsea.ci.upper.robust", 
    "rmsea.pvalue.robust", "srmr", "cfi.robust", "tli.robust"
  )
  
  # 2. Create a named list of your fitted models
  model_list21 <- list(
    "M1: Single-group (Males)"            = fit21_male, 
    "M2: Single-group (Females)"          = fit21_female, 
    "M3: Equal form"                      = fit21_equal, 
    "M4: Equal factor loadings"           = fit21_loadings, 
    "M5: Equal Intercepts"                = fit21_intercepts, 
    "M6: Equal indicator error variances" = fit21_residuals
  ) 
  
  # 3. Extract, combine, and format the data
  fit21_table <- model_list21 %>% 
    # Extract fit measures for each model in the list
    lapply(function(m) fitmeasures(m, selected_indices)) %>% 
    # Bind the vector results into rows of a data frame
    do.call(rbind, .) %>% 
    # Convert to a standard data frame
    as.data.frame() %>% 
    # Move the model names from row names to an actual column
    tibble::rownames_to_column(var = "Model") %>% 
    # Round all numeric metrics to 3 decimal places
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 3))) %>% 
    # Overwrite the chi-square column specifically to 1 decimal place
    mutate(chisq.scaled = round(chisq.scaled, 1)) %>% 
    rename(
      `Chi-Sq (Scaled)` = chisq.scaled,
      `df (Scaled)`     = df.scaled,
      `RMSEA (Robust)`  = rmsea.robust,
      `RMSEA CI LL`     = rmsea.ci.lower.robust,
      `RMSEA CI UL`     = rmsea.ci.upper.robust,
      `RMSEA p-value`   = rmsea.pvalue.robust,
      `SRMR`            = srmr,
      `CFI (Robust)`    = cfi.robust,
      `TLI (Robust)`    = tli.robust
    )
  
  # 4. FIXED: Save the variable AFTER the pipe finishes executing
  saveRDS(fit21_table, file = rds_path_21)
  # message("Generated new fit21 table and saved to RDS file.")
}

# ====================================
# DASS-42, 21 CFA RESULTS TABLE
# ====================================

# Combine the data
combined_models <- rbind(fit42adj_table, fit21_table) %>%
  select(Model, everything()) %>%
  select(-`RMSEA p-value`)

# Generate the structured model fit table
combined_models %>%
  kbl(
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
    booktabs = TRUE #,
    # caption = "Comparison of Model Fit Indices for DASS-42 Adjusted and DASS-21 Models"
  ) %>%
  kable_classic(full_width = FALSE, html_font = "Times New Roman") %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16,
    latex_options = "striped", 
    bootstrap_options = "striped"
  ) %>%
  pack_rows("DASS-42 ADJUSTED MODELS", 1, 6, bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows("DASS-21 MODELS", 7, 12, bold = TRUE)
Model Chi-Sq (Scaled) df (Scaled) RMSEA (Robust) RMSEA CI LL RMSEA CI UL SRMR CFI (Robust) TLI (Robust)
DASS-42 ADJUSTED MODELS
M1: Single-group (Males) 23427.9 811 0.064 0.063 0.065 0.039 0.908 0.903
M2: Single-group (Females) 90173.9 811 0.062 0.062 0.063 0.039 0.911 0.905
M3: Equal form 110540.0 1622 0.063 0.062 0.063 0.039 0.910 0.905
M4: Equal factor loadings 67809.4 1661 0.062 0.062 0.062 0.040 0.910 0.907
M5: Equal Intercepts 110147.4 1703 0.063 0.062 0.063 0.039 0.910 0.909
M6: Equal indicator error variances 69002.8 1745 0.062 0.062 0.062 0.040 0.910 0.911
DASS-21 MODELS
M1: Single-group (Males) 6718.2 186 0.072 0.070 0.073 0.039 0.935 0.926
M2: Single-group (Females) 24891.1 186 0.073 0.072 0.074 0.038 0.931 0.922
M3: Equal form 31119.7 372 0.073 0.072 0.073 0.038 0.932 0.923
M4: Equal factor loadings 20987.1 390 0.071 0.070 0.072 0.039 0.932 0.926
M5: Equal Intercepts 30530.7 411 0.073 0.072 0.074 0.038 0.932 0.931
M6: Equal indicator error variances 21664.8 432 0.071 0.070 0.072 0.039 0.932 0.934

Tests for Independence

Independent samples t-tests were calculated in R using the built in t.test() function. To estimate statistical significance without assuming equal variance, the Welch-Satterthwaite equation was used to calculate degrees of freedom (Satterthwaite, 1946; Welch, 1947). Cohen’s \(d\) was calculated using the effectsize package (Ben-Shachar et al., 2020). Formulas for both functions included the arguments score ~ gender.

Personality Differences

An independent-sample t-test showed small to moderate differences in personality. On average, females \((M = 4.60, SD = 1.20)\) reported higher scores compared to males \((M = 4.37, SD = 1.28)\) on trait agreeableness \((t[13371.8] = 14.48, p <.001, d = .19)\). Conversely, females \((M = 3.13, SD = 1.47)\) reported lower scores compared to males \((M = 3.66, SD = 1.65)\) on trait emotional stability \((t[12861] = -27.07, p <.001, d = -.35)\). A full comparison across each trait can be found in Table 4. These differences are visualized in Fig. 2, which includes trait distributions, average trait differences, and QQ plots separated by gender.

Table 4

Difference in Average Ten-item Personality Inventory Scores by Gender
# 1. Define the traits and display names in sequence
trait_order <- c("openness", "agreeableness", "conscientiousness", "extraversion", "stability")

trait_labels <- c(
  "openness"          = "Openness",
  "agreeableness"     = "Agreeableness",
  "conscientiousness" = "Conscientiousness",
  "extraversion"      = "Extraversion",
  "stability"         = "Emotional Stability"
)

# 2. Reshape and calculate all statistics programmatically
tidy_t_tests <- dass21 %>%
  select(gender, all_of(trait_order)) %>%
  filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female")) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(trait) %>%
  summarise(
    t_obj = list(t.test(score ~ gender, data = pick(everything()), var.equal = FALSE)),
    d_obj = list(cohens_d(score ~ gender, data = pick(everything()))),
    
    n_female  = sum(gender == "Female"),
    m_female  = sprintf("%.2f", mean(score[gender == "Female"])),
    sd_female = sprintf("%.2f", sd(score[gender == "Female"])),
    
    n_male    = sum(gender == "Male"),
    m_male    = sprintf("%.2f", mean(score[gender == "Male"])),
    sd_male   = sprintf("%.2f", sd(score[gender == "Male"])),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    t  = sprintf("%.2f", sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["statistic"]])),
    df = sprintf("%.1f", sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["parameter"]])),
    raw_p = sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["p.value"]]),
    p  = ifelse(raw_p < 0.001, "< .001", sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", raw_p))),
    d  = sprintf("%.2f", sapply(d_obj, function(x) x[["Cohens_d"]]))
  ) %>%
  slice(match(trait_order, trait))

# 3. Manually construct the flat table grid matching the reference image layout
table_rows <- list()
bold_indices <- c() # Keeps track of which rows should be formatted bold
current_row <- 1

for (i in 1:nrow(tidy_t_tests)) {
  data_row <- tidy_t_tests[i, ]
  label_text <- trait_labels[[data_row[["trait"]]]]
  
  # Row A: The main trait header row containing the t-test statistics
  trait_header_row <- data.frame(
    Group_Label = label_text,
    N  = "", M = "", SD = "",
    t  = data_row[["t"]],
    df = data_row[["df"]],
    p  = data_row[["p"]],
    d  = data_row[["d"]],
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  bold_indices <- c(bold_indices, current_row)
  current_row <- current_row + 1
  
  # Row B: Females descriptive statistics row
  female_data_row <- data.frame(
    Group_Label = "  Females",
    N  = as.character(data_row[["n_female"]]),
    M  = data_row[["m_female"]],
    SD = data_row[["sd_female"]],
    t = "", df = "", p = "", d = "",
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  current_row <- current_row + 1
  
  # Row C: Males descriptive statistics row
  male_data_row <- data.frame(
    Group_Label = "  Males",
    N  = as.character(data_row[["n_male"]]),
    M  = data_row[["m_male"]],
    SD = data_row[["sd_male"]],
    t = "", df = "", p = "", d = "",
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  current_row <- current_row + 1
  
  table_rows[[i]] <- bind_rows(trait_header_row, female_data_row, male_data_row)
}

formatted_table_data <- bind_rows(table_rows)

# 4. Render the table matching your exact image layout rules
formatted_table_data %>%
  kbl(
    col.names = c("Variable and Group", "N", "M", "SD", "t", "df", "p", "Cohen's d"),
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
    booktabs = TRUE,
    escape = FALSE #, # Allows the markdown asterisks for italics to render correctly
    # caption = "Independent Samples t-Tests for Personality Variables Split by Gender"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16, 
    html_font = "Times New Roman", 
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
    latex_options = "striped", 
    full_width = TRUE
  ) %>%
  # Match column width distribution percentages
  column_spec(1, width = "30%") %>%
  column_spec(2:8, width = "10%") %>%
  # Keep your top header text perfectly centered
  row_spec(0, align = "c") %>%
  # Apply bold formatting to the trait rows dynamically tracked in Step 3
  row_spec(bold_indices, bold = TRUE)
Variable and Group N M SD t df p Cohen’s d
Openness -12.76 13531.8 < .001 -0.16
Females 30156 4.55 1.32
Males 8679 4.76 1.38
Agreeableness 14.84 13371.8 < .001 0.19
Females 30156 4.60 1.20
Males 8679 4.37 1.28
Conscientiousness 6.84 13523.6 < .001 0.09
Females 30156 4.26 1.47
Males 8679 4.14 1.55
Extraversion 5.95 13516.0 < .001 0.07
Females 30156 3.76 1.25
Males 8679 3.66 1.31
Emotional Stability -27.07 12861.0 < .001 -0.35
Females 30156 3.13 1.47
Males 8679 3.66 1.65
Note. M = Mean; SD = Standard Deviation.

Fig. 2

Personality Difference Plots
# ====================================
# PERSONALITY DENSITY PLOT BY GENDER
# ====================================

personality = dass21 %>% 
  # filter(gender != "Other") %>% 
  select(gender, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, stability, openness) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>% 
  mutate(
    trait = str_replace_all(trait, "stability", "Emotional Stability"),
    trait = str_to_title(trait),
    # Order the plots by factor to match personality diff graphic
    trait = factor(trait, 
                   levels = c("Openness", "Agreeableness", "Conscientiousness", 
                              "Extraversion", "Emotional Stability"))
  ) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = score, fill = gender)) + 
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6, bw = .3, linewidth = .3) + 
  facet_wrap(~trait) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male" = "deepskyblue", "Female" = "darkorange")) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 2), limits = c(0,8)) + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    title = "Personality Trait Distributions by Gender", 
    fill = "Gender"
  ) + 
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Axes
    axis.title = element_blank(), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"), 
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 

    # Legend
    legend.position = "bottom", 
    legend.box = "horizontal", 
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9)
  )

# ====================================
# BARBELL PLOT OF MEAN DIFFERENCES
# ====================================

personality_diff = dass21 %>%
  # filter(gender != "Other") %>%
  select(gender, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, stability, openness) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>%
  mutate(
    trait = str_replace_all(trait, "stability", "emotional stability"),
    trait = str_to_title(trait)) %>%
  group_by(trait, gender) %>%
  summarise(mean_score = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = mean_score) %>%
ggplot(aes(y = reorder(trait, Male))) +
  geom_segment(aes(
    x = Male,  xend = Female, 
    y = trait, yend = trait), 
    color = "darkorange",
    alpha = 0.6
    ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = Male, color = "Male"), 
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6
    ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = Female, color = "Female"), 
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(
    title = "Personality Trait Differences by Gender",
    color = "Avg. Personality Score",
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Male" = "deepskyblue", 
               "Female" = "darkorange")
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 2), limits = c(1,7)) +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), 
    panel.border = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "gray90", linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(), 
    axis.title = element_blank(), 
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),

    # Legend
    legend.position = "bottom", 
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),
    legend.key = element_blank()
    )

combined_personality_plots = personality + personality_diff
combined_personality_plots

# ====================================
# QQ PLOT FEMALE PERSONALITY
# ====================================

fqq_personality = dass21 %>% 
  filter(gender == "Female") %>%
  select(gender, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, stability, openness) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>% 
  mutate(
    trait = str_replace_all(trait, "stability", "Emotional Stability"),
    trait = str_to_title(trait),
    # Order the plots by factor to match personality diff graphic
    trait = factor(trait, 
                   levels = c("Openness", "Agreeableness", "Conscientiousness", 
                              "Extraversion", "Emotional Stability"))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = score)) +
  stat_qq(color = "darkorange", alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ trait) + # Creates individual panels per trait
  labs(
    title = "Normal Q-Q Plots for Personality",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 2), limits = c(-5,5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 12, by = 4), limits = c(-4,12)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), 
    panel.border = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(), 
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
  )

# ====================================
# QQ PLOT MALE PERSONALITY
# ====================================

mqq_personality = dass21 %>% 
  filter(gender == "Male") %>%
  select(gender, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, stability, openness) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "trait", values_to = "score") %>% 
  mutate(
    trait = str_replace_all(trait, "stability", "Emotional Stability"),
    trait = str_to_title(trait),
    # Order the plots by factor to match personality diff graphic
    trait = factor(trait, 
                   levels = c("Openness", "Agreeableness", "Conscientiousness", 
                              "Extraversion", "Emotional Stability"))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = score)) +
  stat_qq(color = "deepskyblue", alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ trait) +
  # labs(
  #   title = "Normal Q-Q Plots for Personality (Males)",
  #   x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
  #   y = "Sample Quantiles"
  # ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 2), limits = c(-5,5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 12, by = 4), limits = c(-4,12)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), 
    panel.border = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(), 
    axis.title = element_blank(), 
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
  )

combined_personality_qq = fqq_personality + mqq_personality
combined_personality_qq

Depression, Anxiety, and Stress Differences

An independent-sample t-test showed small to moderate differences in stress, depression, and anxiety. On average, females \((M = 22.61, SD = 10.70)\) reported higher scores compared to males \((M = 18.92, SD = 10.94)\) on DASS-21 stress \((t[13806.2] = 27.78, p <.001, d = .34)\). The smallest difference reported was on DASS-21 depression \((t[13810.2] = 7.78, p <.001, d = .10)\) where, on average, females \((M = 21.17, SD = 12.68)\) reported higher scores compared to males \((M = 19.95, SD = 12.96)\). Similar to stress, females \((M = 3.13, SD = 1.47)\) reported higher scores compared to males \((M = 3.66, SD = 1.65)\) on DASS-21 anxiety \((t[14612.5] = 26.17, p <.001, d = .31)\). A full comparison across each trait can be found in Table 5. These differences are visualized in Fig. 3, which includes DASS-21 scale distributions, average scale differences, and QQ plots separated by gender.

Table 5

Difference in Total DASS-21 Scores by Gender
# 1. Define the explicit order and subscale matching maps
subscale_order <- c("stress21", "depres21", "anxiet21")

subscale_labels <- c(
  "stress21" = "Stress",
  "depres21" = "Depression",
  "anxiet21" = "Anxiety"
)

# 2. Extract, calculate, and flatten the data grid completely inside one tidy pipe
tidy_table_data <- dass21 %>%
  select(gender, all_of(subscale_order)) %>%
  filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female")) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "subscale", values_to = "score") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(subscale) %>%
  summarise(
    t_obj = list(t.test(score ~ gender, data = pick(everything()), var.equal = FALSE)),
    d_obj = list(cohens_d(score ~ gender, data = pick(everything()))),
    
    n_female  = sum(gender == "Female"),
    m_female  = sprintf("%.2f", mean(score[gender == "Female"])),
    sd_female = sprintf("%.2f", sd(score[gender == "Female"])),
    
    n_male    = sum(gender == "Male"),
    m_male    = sprintf("%.2f", mean(score[gender == "Male"])),
    sd_male   = sprintf("%.2f", sd(score[gender == "Male"])),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    t  = sprintf("%.2f", sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["statistic"]])),
    df = sprintf("%.1f", sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["parameter"]])),
    raw_p = sapply(t_obj, function(x) x[["p.value"]]),
    p  = ifelse(raw_p < 0.001, "< .001", sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", raw_p))),
    d  = sprintf("%.2f", sapply(d_obj, function(x) x[["Cohens_d"]]))
  ) %>%
  group_by(subscale) %>%
  # Flatten the summaries into a 3-row layout per subscale
  reframe(
    Variable_and_Group = c(subscale_labels[subscale], "  Females", "  Males"),
    N  = c("", as.character(n_female), as.character(n_male)),
    M  = c("", m_female, m_male),
    SD = c("", sd_female, sd_male),
    t  = c(t, "", ""),
    df = c(df, "", ""),
    p  = c(p, "", ""),
    d  = c(d, "", "")
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # FIXED: Create a temporary sorting helper column based on our target list rules
  mutate(sort_order = match(Variable_and_Group, subscale_labels)) %>%
  fill(sort_order, .direction = "down") %>%
  # Arrange using the helper variable, then clean up the tracking column
  arrange(sort_order) %>%
  select(-subscale, -sort_order)

# 3. Dynamically find the row locations that should be bold (rows 1, 4, and 7)
bold_row_indices <- which(tidy_table_data[["N"]] == "")

# 4. Render the final table with centered headers and right-aligned cell numbers
tidy_table_data %>%
  kbl(
    col.names = c("Variable and Group", "N", "M", "SD", "t", "df", "p", "Cohen's d"),
    align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"),
    booktabs = TRUE #,
    # caption = "Independent Samples t-Tests for DASS-21 Subscales Split by Gender"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    font_size = 16, 
    html_font = "Times New Roman", 
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), 
    latex_options = "striped", 
    full_width = TRUE
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "30%") %>%
  column_spec(2:8, width = "10%") %>%
  row_spec(0, align = "c") %>%
  row_spec(bold_row_indices, bold = TRUE)
Variable and Group N M SD t df p Cohen’s d
Stress 27.78 13806.2 < .001 0.34
Females 30156 22.61 10.70
Males 8679 18.92 10.94
Depression 7.78 13810.2 < .001 0.10
Females 30156 21.17 12.68
Males 8679 19.95 12.96
Anxiety 26.17 14612.5 < .001 0.31
Females 30156 16.26 10.47
Males 8679 13.04 9.99
Note. M = Mean; SD = Standard Deviation.

Fig. 3

Depression, Anxiety, Stress Difference Plots
# ====================================
# PERSONALITY DENSITY PLOT BY GENDER
# ====================================

dass_gender_dist = dass21 %>% 
  # filter(gender != "Other") %>% 
  select(gender, depres21, stress21, anxiet21) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "scale", values_to = "score") %>%
  mutate(
    scale = str_replace_all(scale, c("anxiet21" = "Anxiety", 
                                     "depres21" = "Depression", 
                                     "stress21" = "Stress")),
    scale = factor(scale, levels = c("Stress", "Depression", "Anxiety"))
    ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = score, fill = gender)) + 
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6, bw = 2, linewidth = .3) + 
  facet_wrap(~scale) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male" = "deepskyblue", "Female" = "darkorange")) + 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, by = 10), limits = c(-5,45)) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  labs(
    title = "DASS Distributions by Gender",
    caption = "Scales ordered high to low by gender differences",
    fill = "Gender"
  ) + 
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0),

    # Axes
    axis.title = element_blank(), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"), 
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 

    # Legend
    legend.position = "bottom", 
    legend.box = "horizontal", 
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9), 
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9)
  )

# ====================================
# BARBELL PLOT OF MEAN DIFFERENCES
# ====================================

dass_gender_diff = dass21 %>%
  # filter(gender != "Other") %>%
  select(gender, depres21, stress21, anxiet21) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "scale", values_to = "score") %>%
  mutate(
    scale = str_replace_all(
      scale, 
      c("anxiet21" = "Anxiety", "depres21" = "Depression", "stress21" = "Stress"))
    ) %>%
  group_by(scale, gender) %>%
  summarise(mean_score = mean(score, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = mean_score) %>%
ggplot(aes(y = reorder(scale, Female))) +
  geom_segment(aes(
    x = Male,  xend = Female,
    y = scale, yend = scale),
    color = "darkorange",
    alpha = 0.6
    ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = Male, color = "Male"),
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6
    ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = Female, color = "Female"),
    size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(
    title = "DASS Differences by Gender",
    caption = "Scales ordered high to low by gender differences",
    color = "Avg. Personality Score",
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Male" = "deepskyblue",
               "Female" = "darkorange")
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 45, by = 10), limits = c(0,45)) +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0),

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(),
    panel.border = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "gray90", linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "black"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),

    # Legend
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "horizontal",
    legend.title = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),
    legend.text = element_text(color = "black", size = 9),
    legend.key = element_blank()
    )

combined_personality_plots = dass_gender_dist + dass_gender_diff
combined_personality_plots

# ====================================
# QQ PLOT FEMALE DASS
# ====================================

fqq_dass = dass21 %>% 
  filter(gender != "Female") %>%
  select(gender, depres21, stress21, anxiet21) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "scale", values_to = "score") %>%
  mutate(
    scale = str_replace_all(scale, c("anxiet21" = "Anxiety", 
                                     "depres21" = "Depression", 
                                     "stress21" = "Stress")),
    scale = factor(scale, levels = c("Stress", "Depression", "Anxiety"))
    ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = score)) +
  stat_qq(color = "darkorange", alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ scale) +
  labs(
    title = "Normal Q-Q Plots for DASS",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 2), limits = c(-5,5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-50, 50, by = 50), limits = c(-10,60)) +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), 
    panel.border = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(), 
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10))
  )

# ====================================
# QQ PLOT MALE DASS
# ====================================

mqq_dass = dass21 %>% 
  filter(gender != "Male") %>%
  select(gender, depres21, stress21, anxiet21) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -gender, names_to = "scale", values_to = "score") %>%
  mutate(
    scale = str_replace_all(scale, c("anxiet21" = "Anxiety", 
                                     "depres21" = "Depression", 
                                     "stress21" = "Stress")),
    scale = factor(scale, levels = c("Stress", "Depression", "Anxiety"))
    ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = score)) +
  stat_qq(color = "deepskyblue", alpha = 0.6, size = 1.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ scale) +
  # labs(
  #   title = "Normal Q-Q Plots for DASS (Males)",
  #   x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
  #   y = "Sample Quantiles"
  # ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-4, 4, by = 2), limits = c(-5,5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-50, 50, by = 50), limits = c(-10,60)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    # Plot Level
    plot.title = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 

    # Panel and Grid Lines
    panel.background = element_blank(), 
    panel.border = element_blank(), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), 

    # Axes
    axis.line = element_blank(), 
    axis.title = element_blank(), 
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
  )

combined_dass_qq = fqq_dass + mqq_dass
combined_dass_qq

Regression

Personality Traits as Predictors of Stress, Depression, and Anxiety

# ====================================
# REGRESSION MODELS: FEMALES
# ====================================

lm_fstress = lm(
  stress21 ~ extraversion + agreeableness + conscientiousness + stability + openness,
  data = dass21 %>% 
    filter(gender == "Female") %>%
    select(stress21, extraversion, agreeableness, conscientiousness, stability, openness)
)

lm_fdepress = lm(
